visual_checks: Look for any indication of session timeout or inactivity timeout behavior on the page
Check if users are warned about the duration of any inactivity timeout at the start of their session or activity
Look for timeout warning messages, banners, or notifications that inform the user how long they have before data loss
Check the login page or session start for information about timeout duration
Verify if data is preserved for at least 20 hours of inactivity (in which case no warning is needed)
Look for timeout information in help text, FAQs, or terms of service links near forms or login areas
pass_conditions: Users are warned of the duration of any inactivity timeout that could cause data loss, at or before the start of the process
Timeout duration information is clearly communicated before the user begins entering data
Data is preserved for at least 20 hours of inactivity, making a timeout warning unnecessary
No inactivity timeout exists that could result in data loss
fail_conditions: An inactivity timeout causes data loss without any prior warning about the timeout duration
Users are not informed of the timeout duration before beginning a data entry process
Timeout warnings appear only after the timeout has already occurred rather than in advance
The timeout duration is mentioned in hard-to-find locations (buried in terms of service) rather than at the point of the activity
Data is lost due to inactivity timeout and the preservation period is less than 20 hours with no warning
na_conditions: Page has no inactivity timeout that could result in data loss
User data is preserved for at least 20 hours of inactivity
Page does not involve data entry or stateful user interactions
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Timeout behavior could not be observed or triggered during evaluation
common_mistakes: Not informing users about session timeout duration before they begin filling out forms
Displaying timeout duration only in a terms-of-service document rather than at the activity start
Warning about an impending timeout only seconds before it occurs rather than proactively at the start
Inconsistent timeout durations across different parts of the application without clear communication
Assuming users will know about timeouts from prior experience rather than explicitly informing them
Using vague timeout warnings (e.g., 'session may expire') without specifying the actual duration

examples.pass: At the start of a tax filing form, a notice states: 'Your session will expire after 30 minutes of inactivity. Please save your progress regularly to avoid data loss.'
examples.fail: A user spends 20 minutes filling out a long application form, and the session silently expires after 15 minutes of inactivity with no prior warning about the timeout duration, resulting in all data being lost
examples.partial: A timeout warning appears in a small banner at the top of the page that is easy to miss, and it only mentions the timeout exists without specifying the exact duration

plain_meaning: If user inactivity could cause data loss, the user must be warned about the inactivity duration -- unless the data is preserved for at least 20 hours. The intent is that users are not surprised by silent data loss.
scope_applies_to: Authenticated sessions where inactivity discards in-progress data
Forms that clear after a period of inactivity
Any feature where walking away for a period destroys work
scope_does_not_apply_to: Content where inactivity does not cause data loss
Systems where data is preserved for 20+ hours regardless
Real-time session expiration warnings (that is SC 2.2.1)
borderline_calibration: A visible notice stating the inactivity duration and data-loss consequence on or near the entry point satisfies the SC.
If data is preserved more than 20 hours, no warning is needed.
The warning need not be dynamic -- a static disclosure is acceptable.
